survivors.desc: Deployed Spirit can REMOVE intent records, superseding the append-only/flag-only constraint. Clean the log by removing (1) stale records superseded by a newer record/INTENT.md/skill that absorbs their substance, and (2) working orders that fail the after-the-task test. Every change needs a justifying psyche statement (a deletion is justified by e.g. a changed mind, no replacement arrow required); removal is psyche-authorized and conservative — when removability is uncertain, flag rather than remove, since over-removal beats under-removal.
survivors.reason: btio and r57r both govern removal/change discipline for the intent layer; merged into one arrow: removal capacity, the two removable classes, the justifying-statement requirement, and the conservative flag-when-uncertain rule.
Distinct arrow: the response to overcapture is the conservative capture gate, not avoiding Spirit. Kept as-is with referents.
Core Spirit design goal: clutter-free curated pristine store. Unique, kept verbatim with referents.
Distinct: guardian judges changes against verbatim psyche words and context; bare affirmations travel with their statement. Kept with referents.

survivors.desc: Reports are a small load-bearing set, not an accumulating archive; history lives in jj/git. Context maintenance reduces report count without losing information by agglomerating per topic: multiple reports become one Refresh report (the Capital variant tag after the number) preserving un-contradicted, un-superseded substance in better form; source reports are then deleted with the Refresh report as landing witness.
survivors.reason: 0r17 and 3ufq describe one arrow — reports as a curated current-state set agglomerated into Refresh reports. Merged preserving the no-archive principle and Refresh-tag mechanics.

survivors.desc: Agglomerating repeated or related intent preserves source provenance and may preserve accumulated importance through weight where that axis exists, but does not automatically raise certainty; certainty rises only when the synthesized statement is itself better supported or stated with higher confidence.
survivors.reason: De-narrated correction (dropped reference to record d5s2) stating the affirmative rule on how agglomeration affects certainty, importance, and provenance.
Distinct mechanism: automated auditor auto-proposes refreshes, psyche confirms source-record retirement. Still-live (auditor role is coming). Kept with referents.
Distinct: refresh/agglomeration is mainly agent behavior trained via skill, with only the relations field as supporting code. Kept with referents.
Distinct: closing duplicate beads preserves all info; competing design ideas kept for comparison. Kept with referents.
Distinct method: fill intent gaps by cross-pollinating analogous patterns from other intent areas. Kept with referents.

survivors.desc: For a prompt addressing multiple lanes, the default capturer is the first responder — in practice the operator (Codex), faster than the designer (Claude) — who writes the Spirit entry; the slower lane then gap-checks and gap-fills only a genuine omission rather than writing a parallel entry. Per psyche 2026-06-05.
survivors.reason: Condensed the one-capturer clarification to its affirmative rule (first responder records, slower lane gap-checks), stripping the narration of the prior backwards draft.

removed.reason: Report-triage protocol (backed-by-intent to architecture; unbacked to question; clear+backed to bead) is settled operating detail now living in INTENT.md/skills; not a durable distinct arrow.
Maintain full constraint tables and audit them — thin operating instruction with no durable load-bearing intent beyond general audit discipline; redundant with audit-against-new-intent practice.
Track intent cut position and hot-topic windows — stale session-bookkeeping instruction, an implementation detail of maintenance tooling rather than a durable intent arrow.

survivors.desc: The Spirit intent log holds only durable psyche statements (Decision/Principle/Correction/Clarification/Constraint) that outlive any single task. Test: would the record still guide after its task is done? Working orders (create-report, dispatch-subagent, audit-X, integrate-branches) are task state; from a task prompt capture only the durable intent within it. A mis-logged working order is flagged as a capture error, not deleted.
survivors.reason: Core durable-vs-working-order capture rule; keep but condense, drop record-number narration and the manifest-to-skills task aside.
Distinct load-bearing 8-level certainty rubric; keep verbatim.

survivors.desc: Spirit capture is a blocking guardian gate, not an advisory check: the guardian vets and admits a proposed record before capture succeeds, resolving or refusing duplicates, contradictions, compounds, and non-intent at the door. An entry and the new referents it introduces are decided as one atomic accept/reject, so no orphan referent survives a refused entry; referent registration is gated the same as records. Intent-resolution operations are typed combined actions the thinker resolves in one atomic call (e.g. add a record while deprecating listed records by identifier).
survivors.reason: Four records circle the same arrow: the guardian as an atomic blocking admission gate over records+referents, plus combined-action resolution. Merge.

survivors.desc: Zero certainty is the removal-candidate state: a Zero record has no value and must not surface by default. The default Observe query excludes Zero; queries take a certainty floor defaulting to Minimum (excludes Zero), and seeing Zero records requires an explicit floor (Zero/Any). This unifies removal-candidate with zero certainty.
survivors.reason: Distinct arrow tying Zero certainty to removal-candidate and default query exclusion; condense.

survivors.desc: Name the persistent typed-record store the SEMA database, not RAD or other nicknames. Sema is the workspace's pure binary domain-tree format; redb-persisted records are conceptually a sema database, holding strings (quotes, names, summaries) today that reduce as natural language becomes typed leaves. Internal database logic uses the same schema-defined message language as component signals, so a growing database component can split into its own daemon without changing the language pattern.
survivors.reason: Both records define the sema-database vocabulary and its schema-message consistency; merge.

survivors.desc: Distinguish a lean-pending-information from a ratified Decision: a psyche statement that leans toward a choice while explicitly asking for more context is a lean (low certainty), not a ratified Decision; conservative capture understates when more-information is flagged. When the psyche marks a new statement low-certainty, search existing records on that topic and split or correct any higher-certainty record so high- and low-certainty parts are represented separately. Spirit clients do not provide capture time.
survivors.reason: Three capture-discipline corrections about certainty honesty and lean-vs-ratified; merge, drop record-number narration.

survivors.desc: The Spirit RecordQuery composes filter dimensions: TopicSelection (multi-topic, partial=any-of or full=all-of, plus topic-catalog queries), optional Kind, CertaintySelection (filter by certainty/magnitude to select e.g. removal candidates), and a first-class RecencySelection (Any, Newest N, Since identifier/timestamp, Window range). Also support numeric-identifier lookup (exact and ranges), qualitative depth requests (deeper/very-deep) instead of exact counts, public-only/private-only shortcut verbs, and an explicit privacy-query subtype exposing at-most/equal/at-least selectors while normal queries stay Zero-only.
survivors.reason: Eight overlapping query-feature decisions all describe one composable Spirit query surface; merge into one capabilities arrow. Verified every arrow preserved: shortcut verbs, numeric-id lookup, privacy selectors, multi-topic, recency, certainty filter, topic-catalog, qualitative depth.

survivors.desc: The cloud daemon starts almost-stateless and caches last-known-state of queried provider (Cloudflare) resources; cache loss is acceptable because the provider is the source of truth. The first cache is runtime/volatile (in-memory), provider-backed and possibly lossy; persistent storage is deferred until state worth preserving emerges.
survivors.reason: Three records describe one cloud-cache stance: volatile, provider-authoritative, persistence deferred; merge.

survivors.desc: Real-time streaming is a new Signal capability (working name signal-real-time): it is Signal, not Sema, and its storage format is open (likely a native real-time format rather than Sema).
survivors.reason: Both records define the signal-real-time capability and its Signal-not-Sema nature; merge.

survivors.desc: Schema source lowering rejects duplicate declarations for the same type name: when an inline declaration and another inline or trailing namespace declaration both introduce a name, it is an error, not an order-dependent override or merge.
survivors.reason: Distinct schema-lowering constraint; condense, keep.

survivors.desc: A registered but undelegated domain name returns a typed no-records result.
survivors.reason: Small distinct query-semantics constraint about undelegated domains; keep terse.

survivors.desc: NOTA's bracket-only string form makes any NOTA expression embedding-safe and escape-free inside every double-quote host (JSON, Rust raw strings, Nix, YAML, TOML, shell args, HTTP bodies, DB columns, env vars, XML attrs): NOTA never emits a double-quote. NOTA is the carrier; the schema at each embedding site supplies meaning.
survivors.reason: Both express the carrier-vs-interpretation arrow: NOTA is the universal escape-free payload in double-quote hosts. Merged and trimmed.

survivors.desc: NOTA structs are positional: position plus the read-time schema encodes meaning, no field-name tags. Plain PascalCase tokens are unit variants; parenthesized variants carry data. Every field is always present, with explicit None for an optional value (Spirit privacy comes from public/private shorthand that lowers to the full record, not omitted fields).
survivors.reason: Core positional-encoding rules: no tags, unit vs data variants, no omitted fields. One arrow about NOTA record shape. Bumped importance — positional-no-tags is load-bearing.
Distinct rule: unspaced multi-word string atoms are camelCase mirroring PascalCase variants, never kebab-case. Kept.

survivors.desc: NOTA string delimiters are required only for strings with spaces or forbidden symbols; bare-eligible strings (topic names, symbol-safe schema and type names) stay bare atoms.
survivors.reason: Both: bare atoms unless delimiters needed; x020 is the symbol-name special case of 0dsr. Merged.

survivors.desc: The four-character bracket-pipe delimiter is for pretty indented multiline string blocks with common indentation stripped on parse, not the ordinary inline delimiter. Keep one pipe-text shape and escape literal close-marker text rather than widening fences.
survivors.reason: Both about the pipe/bracket-pipe multiline codec: its purpose and its escape behavior. Merged.

survivors.desc: The NOTA brace {} is a strict key-value map: every entry is exactly a key plus a value, no single-token entries. Key-value-ness is low-level NOTA structure (macros may consume it at schema positions). Keys form a typed enum where case plus sigil selects the variant and dispatches the value type. Namespace body is {TypeName TypeDefinition}; struct body is {fieldName TypeRef}; derived names use a value-side marker like (Derive).
survivors.reason: Both define the brace as a strict typed key-value map; 1rci is the structural-vs-macro clarification of ghw7. Merged, trimmed.

survivors.desc: Square brackets are NOTA's raw vector container delimiter, not the schema syntax for declaring a Vec; declare a vector type with an explicit parenthesized typed object (Vec of X). At a typed position expecting String or a string newtype, bracket content reads as string data (a string is a vector of characters), so [] is not always a vector.
survivors.reason: All three concern square-bracket semantics: raw vector vs Vec declaration vs string-at-typed-position. Merged.
Distinct division of labor: NOTA owns raw structure/serialization plus None/(Some x); Schema owns the entire type-name vocabulary. Kept.

survivors.desc: schema-next needs a Bytes primitive (ideally fixed-size byte arrays) beyond String/Integer/Boolean/Path, so binary values (BLS keys, signatures, digests, fingerprints, nonces) type as bytes. It also needs a hash-identifier type whose value is bytes but whose NOTA projection is a canonical short string code, generalizing the 12-byte base36 RecordIdentifier.
survivors.reason: Both add byte-typed primitives to schema-next; lm84 is the hash-identifier specialization. Merged.

survivors.desc: When a data-carrying variant's payload is a single-use enum, inline it as a bracket list at the variant position — (Busy [DatabaseOverloaded ResourceDisconnected OtherReason]) — rather than naming an enum and wrapping. Name it separately when reused, semantically independent, large (10+ variants), or part of a stable cross-component contract.
survivors.reason: Distinct no-wrappers inlining pattern with clear inline-vs-name criteria. Condensed.

survivors.desc: A .schema file reads in a known Schema context: its root struct type is implied by the extension/filename (like Rust modules), so fields appear positionally with no outer wrapper. The root holds a key-value map of datatype names to raw NOTA objects, first field the imports/exports namespace (brace map) then input/output struct(s) in square brackets, combinable. Reading needs both meta-schema and concrete schema through the same self-describing format; authored data files prefer typed NOTA whose filename defines the root type.
survivors.reason: All express the file-context-implies-root-type arrow: no outer wrapper, root from filename, root field layout, meta+concrete self-describing, typed-NOTA-by-filename. Merged.

survivors.desc: The resolved assembled-schema artifact is the Asschema (.asschema) file: pure NOTA holding fully resolved enum and struct definitions with no remaining macros, read against the known Asschema root struct positionally with no outer wrapper. Human-readable debug output is not .asschema and must be named as a witness.
survivors.reason: All three define the .asschema artifact: what it is, its no-wrapper root reading, and the witness boundary. Merged.

survivors.desc: Schema headers are ordered enum-root declarations (a vector of ordered enum variants), not namespace vectors. Assembled root syntax carries Input and Output bodies directly in their known positions or via a real common enum object, never wrapped in meaningless one-field structs added to satisfy vector syntax.
survivors.reason: All three about the header/root being ordered enum declarations and rejecting one-field wrapper structs. Merged.

survivors.desc: Plural record replies (e.g. RecordsObserved) expose the vector directly in the signal tree rather than wrapping it in a single-field record that nests delimiters before the vector.
survivors.reason: Distinct signal-tree reply-shape arrow against single-field wrapping of vectors. Kept, de-negated.

removed.reason: Process reminder to use latest accepted syntax and treat denied patterns as stale — transient agent-discipline, no durable distinct arrow.
Marks a specific dated report's syntax obsolete — settled stale-example correction tied to a past artifact.
Vague aspiration that ordered-vector-of-boxes NOTA notation deserves its own library; no load-bearing decision retained.

One-line speculation that NOTA shape can project through front matter; the general escape-free-embedding principle (7y8w/mrcx) already covers NOTA-in-any-double-quote-host, so no unique still-relevant arrow is lost.

survivors.reason: Unique still-relevant arrow: privileged meta-signal Import that bypasses guardian for re-import into a fresh store, while the working signal stays gated. Kept verbatim.

survivors.desc: Migration is a logged fold: replay the previous store's versioned operation log through the version From-chain into a store at the next schema, recording a typed migration entry — retiring copy-everything binaries. Per version-boundary hand-written Rust bridges handle moved/transformed types; auto-migration runs once on DB load when the version marker lags, then updates and persists it. Hash-identity migration builds a hash-to-former-ordinal mapping table while live lookup moves to hash identity.
survivors.reason: Merge: t0tu (fold/logged replay), zf4v (the upgrade/bridge/auto-migrate procedure), xfc5 (hash-identity migration table). Unified as the single migration-mechanism arrow. Desc trimmed to ~280 chars and kept affirmative.

survivors.desc: Sema-upgrade is the universal stateful schema-upgrade component for sema databases (persona-component typed-record stores). It boots FIRST as engine-pre-zero infrastructure owned by the engine manager, before any persona daemon, and checks each daemon's stored schema-version-hash against the hash its code declares. The SEMA interface owns transitory dual-database concurrency during a live transition. First test case: treat the legacy intent log as a 0.01 spirit database; the schema-spec-language eventually drives transforms from declarative diffs.
survivors.reason: Merge: 64ug (seed: component for updating sema DBs), tmji (full definition + legacy-intent test case), 2ypu (boots first, version-hash check), qvyc (transitory dual-DB concurrency in SEMA). One arrow. Desc trimmed toward ~280 chars and kept affirmative.
Distinct detail of the dual-version upgrade reply contract (distinguish old-DB vs new-DB write failures) not captured in the consolidated sema-upgrade definition. Kept verbatim.
Distinct arrow: schema diffs infer standard migrations but ambiguous transforms need explicit annotations/traits. Drives the declarative-diff direction; kept verbatim.
Unique arrow: the closed data-lifecycle ladder (nominate, tombstone, archive, collect, compact, purge) as a named set on the schema-derived spirit. Kept verbatim.
Unique arrow: archive semantics are redirect-forward-only, not migrate-forward. Distinct from migration mechanism. Kept verbatim.
Distinct constraint: default profile must not cut to a new versioned DB until records are migrated/reachable; empty side-by-side daemon is test-only, not the production surface. Kept verbatim.

Distinct mandatory-cascade principle: every component storing ARCA-derived types migrates its own DB when the format changes. Names ARCA specifically; not covered by sema-upgrade arrow. Kept verbatim.
Distinct storage-format rule: identifier-migration data is stored as NOTA, not SEMA (SEMA is schema/engine structure). Kept verbatim.

removed.reason: Restates the global no-backward-compatibility / best-design-only hard override (already in AGENTS.md) scoped to migrations; adds no durable migration-specific arrow.
Two-deploy-stack transient detail (main can skip ops it cannot process; dev does the op; partial-support is an open follow-up). Settled/transient implementation guidance with low durability.

survivors.desc: Schema declares data types only — no effects, fan-out targets, effect tables, or storage descriptors. Effects are runtime dispatch and logic, outside schema's lane.
survivors.reason: Core load-bearing arrow: schema scope is data types only. De-negated and stripped of agent-drift narration.

survivors.desc: NOTA + schema is pure data specification, more specific than Rust (which mixes logic with data), like CapnProto's own spec language. They are the deliberate half-step from text-as-pseudocode toward Sema, the fully-correct system where code and data are typed structure. NOTA always corresponds to a specified object; the chain runs all the way down — schema is data, specifies data types, emits Rust object shapes, and operating on those via SEMA can edit the schema.
survivors.reason: Three records express one arrow: why NOTA+schema exist as a typed data specification language and the full correspondence chain to Sema.
Distinct arrow: DomainScope must be a typed nested prefix of the Domain enum, not a string vector.

survivors.desc: The intent unit is the domain: a specific grounded subject grouped under broad areas (terms category and topic retired). Domain is a closed enum with a broad category-theory starting set, represented as grouped schema enums by area (e.g. Home carrying Housing, Maintenance, and siblings) rather than one flat enum or free-text.
survivors.reason: Three records circle one arrow: domain as the closed, grouped-enum intent unit replacing category/topic.

survivors.desc: Spirit entries carry exactly two orthogonal axes: Certainty (confidence the statement is true) and Importance (how much it matters). They are never conflated and neither substitutes for the other — a tentative idea can be very-low certainty yet high importance. Importance (formerly Weight) is declared directly by the psyche, not only accumulated through reinforcement; a statement made once can carry high importance. No separate Weight field.
survivors.reason: Four records all express the certainty/importance two-axis model and the Weight->Importance rename.

survivors.desc: If a separate weight axis is added, it uses the same qualitative Magnitude ladder (Zero through Maximum) on a second axis distinct from certainty — not an integer count — keeping the contract all-qualitative; weight would preserve accumulated importance across agglomeration after source mentions vanish. Low-certainty design: production needs no immediate weight migration; weight stays in future record-shape design until the mechanism settles.
survivors.reason: Three weight-mechanism records merge into the still-open weight design arrow, kept distinct from the settled importance arrow.

survivors.desc: Universal Magnitude type in the shared typed-record crate (signal-core/signal-sema) replaces all small ordinal enums (e.g. Certainty). Eight variants: Zero, Minimum, VeryLow, Low, Medium, High, VeryHigh, Maximum; Zero (the neutral absent rung, chosen over Option/None) declared first so derived Ord places it lowest. Fixed-byte rkyv discriminant makes width free; consumption subset is per-component policy. Spirit reads certainty=Zero as removal-candidate; certainty stays optional at the persona-spirit entry boundary with absence semantics local to Spirit rather than a None inside shared vocabulary.
survivors.reason: Four records define the Magnitude type, its Zero bottom rung, and Spirit's local certainty-absence policy.

survivors.desc: Spirit record identity is a stable, non-reusable, opaque random handle assigned once at creation, frozen and never reused (not a content-address fingerprint, since records are mutable, and not reusable incremental numbers). Concretely: 96-bit CSPRNG values rendered as lowercase base36 shortest-unique-prefix codes, minimum three characters per record kind. Recency tracking uses daemon-stamped time, not the id.
survivors.reason: Four records all specify Spirit record identity as a stable random opaque handle.

survivors.desc: Provenance and agglomeration are expressed through a relations field on records — a vector of short non-colliding record-id hashes pointing to source/related records (a Correction relates to what it corrects) — not a dedicated Composite record type. This lets repeated or related intent be agglomerated into a newer, stronger record without losing provenance; refreshing several intents may yield one merged record or two-or-three records of different kinds.
survivors.reason: Three records converge: provenance via relations field, no Composite type.

survivors.desc: Define the ASSEMBLED schema first: the canonical, macro-free, sugar-free FINAL data model Rust is generated from — processable as ordinary data, not a hidden parser control path. TypeReference is a sum (Plain, Vector, Map, Optional); TypeDeclaration is a sum (Struct, Enum, Newtype) — all final forms. It is a typed Rust data model with a NOTA projection, carrying fully-qualified module-unique names so it can reference types across modules/crates. It strictly and faithfully represents data as stored: load-bearing field order stays visible; use the real product/sum/map/scalar shape, never homogeneous containers for heterogeneous fields or empty wrapper records. Lock the assembled model first; design surface sugar only after the target exists.
survivors.reason: Six records define the assembled-schema concept (assembled-first, data-first macro-free, Rust model with NOTA projection, qualified names, order-faithful, truthful representation).

survivors.desc: In the assembled schema a struct is a key-value brace map: field name is the key, type reference is the value — the long-form that struct sugar expands into. Field names and type names are unified: a field's identity IS its type name, derived once and lowered to lowercase (camelCase) in code while the type stays PascalCase (struct Entry's Kind reference -> field kind: Kind). Type declarations are visibility-tagged Public(name,value) or Private(name,value): Public is exported for library use; Private, local, and inline are not. An inline PascalCase type in a field is sugar — derive the field name from the type, log the inline type as a Private local declaration (module-local non-exported Rust type).
survivors.reason: Struct-as-brace-map, field-name=type-name, visibility tags, inline-type sugar all describe the assembled-schema struct/field model.

survivors.desc: A newtype in the assembled schema is a single-element brace with just the wrapped type — (Public Topic { String }), not a derived field name. TypeValue carries a distinct Newtype(TypeReference) variant: brace with one element lowers to Newtype; alternating name-type pairs lower to Struct. Rust emits a tuple-struct pub struct Topic(pub String) with NotaTransparent (Topic([foo]) and topic.0). Surface @-syntax: Topic@String or Topic@{ String }.
survivors.reason: Distinct, detailed newtype representation decision. Condensed mini-essay, dropped provenance narration.

survivors.desc: Assembled-schema scalar built-ins are named in full English: Integer, Boolean, String — never Bool, U64, or abbreviations. Any further scalar built-ins follow the same full-English rule. Text is reserved for a possible future string-with-spaces variant; String is the scalar in use now.
survivors.reason: Distinct arrow applying the full-English identifier rule to the scalar floor. Condensed.

survivors.desc: The schema language handles only the workspace Rust subset: named-field structs, named-variant enums (bare, data-carrying, struct-variant, nested), newtype (single-unnamed-field) structs, and scalar primitives (unit enums, booleans, integers, strings, vectors). No tuples — tuples are poorly-defined structs and excluded; no multi-field tuple structs, anonymous tuples, or field-less anonymous types. Strings are conceptually vectors of characters behind boxed storage; domain concepts should be typed beyond raw String (typed-string newtypes), and EnumIdentifier is a core constrained-string type.
survivors.reason: b05y (no-tuples Rust subset), afa8 (scalars + typed strings), cn5a (EnumIdentifier) all describe the schema type vocabulary.

survivors.desc: Composite type constructors are NOTA-layer datatype objects that schema reads, not schema-only sugar: the collection macros are Vec, Option, and KeyValue (not Map — risks reading as a verb — nor KeyValueMap; KeyValue is followed by key type then value type). Vector sugar is structurally a single-field container object. NOTA does not own type declarations; schema does — plain NOTA / type-reference positions can name scalars or composites (String, Vec) without declaring a new type, while declarations use positional struct, enum, and newtype forms.
survivors.reason: 2dzp (KeyValue naming), ewo6 (NOTA composite layer), f743 (schema owns declarations), wtaz (vector = single-field container) all describe composite-type constructors.
Distinct mandatory two-layer enum-of-enums structure rule. Kept as-is with referents.

survivors.desc: A schema declares its roots at one designated point only — the named entry-point set generalizing fixed input/output into a roots collection; nesting adds no further roots. Everything else is a free datatype declared in the namespace and referenced by roots at some position. This shapes the assembled schema as a roots section plus a free-datatype-declarations section, matching the operator roots model.
survivors.reason: i8wt and its gap-fill restatement p02v define the roots-plus-free-datatypes model.

survivors.desc: A schema root header is a bare list of PascalCase names for Input and Output roots — each name is both the operation/reply vocabulary and an exported top-level type that library consumers import and that resolves to a real named type defined in the namespace or inline. Headers list exported variant object names directly with payload shape resolved from the namespace, rather than repeating each variant's payload as a parenthesized pair. The header declaration is a vector of ordered enum variants / namespace nodes, not one payload pair per verb.
survivors.reason: Five records all describe the bare-name root header that exports types and resolves payloads from namespace.

survivors.desc: Header declarations come first and drive receive/dispatch triage, representing the outermost shape. Headers should be derivable from the actual assembled data type structure rather than authored in a separate place. Schema entry points usually begin with an enum-like variant decision because the first structural match creates the routing namespace, mirroring the binary signal header. Schema creates base Input and Output enums for actor reaction types; execution logic matches these variants to choose reply and state action.
survivors.reason: m76h, nob0, rzsx, r1un all describe header-driven dispatch and base I/O enums.

survivors.desc: Schema has a sectioned three-part structure (parts 2-3 optionally recursive): (1) SPECIFYING — imports and exports / name-sharing; (2) INPUT — operations and queries received; (3) OUTPUT — responses emitted. Each part's first sub-part is a header (or derived from the assembled schema). Per component the struct order is: optional metadata/imports, then three short headers (regular signal, owner signal, sema operation) each as a vector of variants, then namespace map, optionally a final extension vector. The top-level outer vector holds distinct typed sub-vector sections (e.g. messaging objects, then type namespace), not one flat mixed vector; roots are positional structs using square brackets since structs are ordered field vectors.
survivors.reason: rmqo, sdgj, gm3a, eb5l, ba22, 2szc, 7se2 all describe schema section structure (3-part, per-component field order, typed top-level sections, signal/owner/sema headers).

survivors.desc: Schema namespaces use maps reserved for true name-value namespaces; keys and fields provide names, and namespace order supports linear loading. Schema grows nested type namespaces mirroring Rust modules (e.g. Envelope.Destination) so role-local names exist without polluting one global namespace, with serialized references using a string-level delimiter (e.g. colon). Namespaces can import local type namespaces from other schemas; imports use explicit import variants (not an opaque Path-with-optional-selection), and the import section is a key map whose values are import variants. Other extensible shape favors enums and vectors.
survivors.reason: c8lc, ktmd, l1qe, mr16, nh8y, ucqp, q4b3, tzp3 all describe schema namespaces and imports.

survivors.desc: Schema inline declarations inside root input/output payloads stay shallow — inline bodies for operation-level shape, deeper reusable or nested structure moved into named namespace types rather than buried in root vectors. Inline declarations are promoted into the source type scope: later inline references (Topic *) resolve to an earlier inline Topic String, and library consumers see promoted types through the namespace/export surface without duplicate trailing namespace entries.
survivors.reason: tbff (inline promotion to scope) and yntp (keep inline shallow) both govern inline declarations in root payloads.

survivors.desc: Schema is purely positional: a name appears only where something is genuinely keyed (a namespace map entry by its key, a sum variant by its tag); a positional slot — a struct's fields, the Input/Output enum positions — is bare because position already says what it is. Struct bodies are positional lists of field types and reject the old field-Type pair syntax; an explicitly different field name uses the dot differentiator (source.TypeReference), but dot syntax is invalid when the explicit role equals the type-derived name (Entry topic.Topic rejected, use Entry Topic).
survivors.reason: 6wwf, lpk9, i3p0 all express the positional/no-labels rule and its dot exception.

survivors.desc: Schema files carry structure, not explanatory comments — declarations are positional and self-explanatory through the type names they declare; explanatory prose lives in the Rust implementation files.
survivors.reason: bw9v and mkts both say schema files carry no comments.

survivors.desc: The schema for Signal/Nexus/Sema planes is the REACTIVE SCHEMA, an extension of a simpler BASE SCHEMA (root struct plus namespace of type definitions, pure data specification). Plain schema says what data IS; reactive schema adds the reaction surface (input in, nexus through, sema state, output back) and says how data MOVES — analogous to Nix's built-in derivation extended by higher-level builders. Most schemas are plain; a component top-level schema is reactive. The root schema must itself be described by an explicit schema object — the .schema reader may know it reads the root Schema type, but Schema still needs a concrete schema of its own fields. Syntax details (namespace ordering, rooting, field-name derivation) remain open.
survivors.reason: mimk and xzlv both name the reactive-vs-base schema distinction; y1g8 adds the self-describing root Schema.

survivors.desc: Plane payload types shed their plane prefix: the per-plane envelope (Signal, Nexus, or Sema) carries the plane, so payloads are named just Input and Output, scoped to plane modules (rename SemaInput/SemaOutput/NexusInput/NexusOutput to plane-module Input/Output), naming the plane once. Because a plain vector denotes homogeneous elements, the assembled root must not show differently-typed Input and Output as bare sibling vector elements — it uses a true common wrapper or a struct/field shape preserving their distinct types.
survivors.reason: 7118 (plane-prefix shedding, DRY Input/Output) and 75ea (root can't be homogeneous vector of differently-typed I/O) are tightly coupled root-shape decisions.

survivors.desc: Spirit defines a small-record type carrying core load-bearing fields — identifier, topics, kind, description summary, magnitude, daemon-stamped date/time — distinct from the full record's metadata. Short-form reads (Recent, Today) and CollectRemovalCandidates emit it; archiving and downstream tools consume it. The observation mode emitting these fields should be named to match its data (DescriptionOnly is misleading). Reply schemas avoid nested summary-shaped acknowledgements, and outcomes (archive-created, archive-appended, backup-archive-used) report as typed variants, not string messages.
survivors.reason: 8rew, jwgm, 8l6x, c782 all shape Spirit's reply/small-record surface.

survivors.desc: Typed end-to-end, never string-keyed: extend the typed model first (e.g. add a real NodeService enum variant with NotaEncode/Decode in the horizon model), author the fact, project typed, consume typed — string keys, or-empty-list defaults, and fixture-fed checks bypass the typed pipeline. Typed-record vocabulary is trees of branches (variable-size or data-carrying: vectors, data-carrying enum variants, structs with variable members) and leaves (fixed-size terminals like unit-variant enums). SymbolPath is a flat vector of Name segments recovering role from schema position. A pure-projection library (Horizon, no runtime triad) needs a types-only-module schema variant so datatypes generate without forcing an Input/Output signal plane. SignalCore holds workspace-universal core types in the 64-bit namespace partition; each component contributes its own zone.
survivors.reason: qkvx, hfvl, aflu, vu2k, isvp all express typed-end-to-end data modeling discipline.

survivors.desc: Every component repository gets a concept schema file starting at version 0.1; all components need schemas, with Spirit, Orchestrate, Mind, and Persona as preferred pilots. Schema files create the data types for the libraries they serve, remain the source of data-type truth, and may live beside Rust source when simplest. Schema files can be split by contract or schema-file type so parsing is per-file and positional over root NOTA objects rather than one overloaded monolith.
survivors.reason: ddlv, 5ew0, 0be2, 0q0u all govern schema-file rollout and layout.

survivors.desc: The canonical stored Spirit record stays one uniform shape — no per-kind storage variants. A view of a record or a creation shorthand would be a presentation/ergonomics layer over the uniform record, not the stored shape; whether such a view is useful is undecided.
survivors.reason: Distinct arrow: uniform stored record shape, views are separate. Condensed, dropped record-number narration.

survivors.desc: Separate a thing's categorical KIND (one-of-N essence with no off) from its additive CAPABILITY vector (the things it does). Kind is an explicit categorical choice with no implicit defaults — mutually-exclusive, validated at projection with a typed error — never modelled as pre-selected defaults an additive vector overrides, since an additive vector cannot express un-selection. Additive vectors for what you turn on; categorical labels for the one-of-N essence.
survivors.reason: Distinct, broadly applicable modeling principle: categorical kind vs additive capability vector. Condensed.

survivors.desc: Spirit topics are user-creatable single strings — broad atomic single-word concepts, not compound hyphenated phrases (prefer two topics [intent logging] over [intent-log]); a multi-concept record uses multiple topic words. Reuse existing topic words when they cover the substance; invent new ones only when none fits, to keep records discoverable by either concept and avoid near-synonym vocabulary explosion. Spirit entries carry one clarified description and no verbatim field.
survivors.reason: uara, pnt9, 5ip8 all shape Spirit topic/description fields.
Distinct settled vocabulary: Shallow/Recent/Deep/VeryDeep depth scope with target counts. Kept as-is.
Minor but distinct: schema examples should show multi-variant headers.

survivors.desc: Component Sema databases (daemon durable state) get native version-controlled server-backed atomic durability with no state loss, built once as a reusable library of generic types and traits every component can opt into. Dolt-informed; strict-typed hard-migration-per-schema-change is the core constraint, exact generic mechanism settled by prior-art research.
survivors.reason: j487 explicitly extends 29pb; both describe the version-controlled server-backed atomic durability mechanism as a reusable library.

survivors.desc: Schema is the macro-language source of truth for component data, wire, storage, and upgrade behavior. All Spirit, Signal, Nexus, and SEMA operations are declared in schema files first; generated schema types are the source of every operation data type, and handwritten Rust only implements behavior on those generated nouns.
survivors.reason: Both state schema-as-source-of-truth; 5hjv specializes to operations declared in schema first.

survivors.desc: Schema migration is a workspace-wide structural prerequisite for every deployed persona triad (not just spirit): a contract or storage-schema change requires the running daemon and its redb to migrate coherently. The deploy-restart-update flow means a daemon meeting an existing redb must find no drift or have a sema-upgrade path; without it, any contract edit after first deploy breaks the next restart.
survivors.reason: c9fv states migration is a real per-triad concern; veqq states it is the structural prerequisite for deployment via the restart flow.

survivors.desc: Spirit gets an explicit capability to remove intent entries beyond supersession/negation, plus first-class removal-candidate review: a collect-removal-candidates observation flow that uses record certainty/magnitude to surface low-confidence records and archives or emits reviewed Zero-certainty records before retracting them from the hot store.
survivors.reason: All three describe Spirit's record-removal capability: the remove operation, the review flow, and the collect-and-archive variant.

survivors.desc: Upgrade mechanisms are live typed SEMA operations on the Asschema: a protocol or database-format change is a typed operation/message, applied as database work and used as source for derived datatype and upgrade/compatibility code. The same library handles record-level and schema-changing operations. Diff operations have three families: Add, Remove, Modify (Modify subdivides into ContainerEmbed, EnumWrap, Reorder, KeyChange).
survivors.reason: py4h/1wn5/z550/wv7w all frame upgrades as typed SEMA operations on the schema; 8cs3 enumerates the diff-operation families that make them concrete.

survivors.desc: Schema diffs drive per-type upgrade trait requirements, compile-time-optionally per main-next pair: unchanged types emit no code, changed types need hand-written upgrade behavior, database load runs needed upgrades, old-version messages are upgraded/accepted/logged. Upgrade knowledge belongs to next, which declares the next schema crate as a Cargo dependency so the macro sees both schemas and emits the VersionProjection.
survivors.reason: All four cover diff-driven upgrade-code generation: diff requirements, compile-time-optional per pair, ownership by next version, and the cargo-dependency emission mechanism.

survivors.desc: Workspace-wide version vocabulary: NEXT = the in-progress version being authored (authors always write from next's view); MAIN = the current published canonical baseline, imported as comparison point; PREVIOUS/LAST = the prior iteration of any contract, schema, message format, or data shape. If main == next nothing changed and no upgrade path compiles; if main != next the machinery generates the From-chain from main to next.
survivors.reason: Canonical next/main/previous vocabulary plus the main==next no-upgrade development model; load-bearing and distinct.

survivors.desc: The schema component is self-editing: the schema daemon IS the editor of its Asschema, receiving upgrade messages and deriving both new data-type code and upgrade-migration code. It has three faces from one NOTA source: runtime daemon (serves header-to-schema lookups, resolves schemas/namespaces/core definitions/macro libraries with in-memory caching), library crate (schema types other crates import), and macro substrate (consumed by signal- and sema-side macros at build time).
survivors.reason: xbu8 (self-editing daemon), z8gj (three faces), v1ya (daemon resolves/caches schema material) all describe the schema component's runtime/library/macro roles.

survivors.desc: rkyv's storage layout reserves more namespace than data needs (a bool occupies a byte; a small enum's discriminator fits in a byte). This headroom enables zero-cost migration-free schema changes: adding a unit variant that still fits the byte, appending struct fields under append-only encoding, widening fixed-width ints, provided variant order is preserved. The schema-layout schema exposes this headroom so derivation can skip zero-cost changes. Cap'n-Proto-style structural-compatibility discipline lives at the rkyv layer, not the NOTA text layer.
survivors.reason: gvgu corrects where the Cap'n Proto discipline lives (rkyv not NOTA); ux4w details the same rkyv byte headroom and zero-cost change class.

survivors.desc: Each persona contract carries an explicit content-addressable schema-layout schema in a NOTA-based language: its hash is its identity, so any edit changes the address (the version). The version-checking pipeline detects schema-address mismatches between code and stored data, walks the diff, and derives per-type migration operations (zero-cost variant extensions, append-only fields, structural migrations) baked into how types are written. MigrationIndex is the runtime decoder lookup; migration is per type; upgrade and downgrade are one compatibility-projection relation.
survivors.reason: wrjl is the content-addressable schema-layout + diff-derived migration design; wsgh (MigrationIndex), 1352 (per-type), b1c4 (up/down one relation) are its mechanism details.

survivors.desc: Persona daemon schema migration uses in-process versioned reads: every record carries a schema-version tag, the daemon links every prior version's types and dispatches read-side on the tag, migrating older records on read (optionally rewriting), zero downtime per bump, applied to every triad. The per-type Migration trait carries a self-check/peer-coordination message knowing whether to consult the next-version daemon over an explicit version-handover protocol. Pilot record-shape changes are clean-break; production migration is operator work at cutover.
survivors.reason: w1ss (in-process versioned reads), 00zx (Migration trait peer-check), 0wr7 (explicit daemon handover protocol), o7lx (pilot clean-break vs operator production migration) all describe the runtime migration mechanism.

survivors.desc: Schema namespace additions follow append-only discipline (new names added at the end of the available namespace) and enum slot planning minimizes database rewrites, so future enum-like compilation and upgrade logic stays compatible across upgrades.
survivors.reason: Both are append-only/enum-slot planning to minimize rewrites across compatible upgrades.

survivors.desc: Spirit releases carry schema-aware version numbers, and deployed Spirit versions are tagged before schema-migration planning.
survivors.reason: Both concern schema-aware versioning/tagging of Spirit releases ahead of migration.

survivors.desc: Components have two schema categories. EXTERNAL schemas describe surfaces outside daemon runtime: wire contracts to other components (signal-vocabulary surviving the socket boundary) and database/storage contracts (state surviving process exit). INTERNAL schemas describe surfaces within runtime: actor message vocabularies and inter-actor channel contracts sharing process memory. Multiple schemas per component, each describing ONE channel; major actors get a schema. Internal-schema changes break neither database nor wire.
survivors.reason: Distinct external/internal schema taxonomy (one channel per schema); de-narrated from the record-659 reference.

survivors.desc: Domain relations live as first-class, highly visible schema-declared vocabulary in a dedicated taxonomy schema surface, a new schema interface kind alongside the plane schemas, not an ad-hoc data file. The relation and expansion mechanism is reusable across components such as the mind.
survivors.reason: Distinct high-importance arrow: domain relations as a dedicated reusable taxonomy schema surface.
Distinct stance: recompiling to change enum sets is trivial and zero-downtime upgrade is a goal, so schema/enum changes are not a cost to design around.
Distinct upgrade testing pipeline: minimal DB then disposable live-DB copy before accepting a schema change.
Distinct: per-repo signal-X versioned schema libraries enable cross-version decoding and recovery.
Distinct: schema and upgrade work carries explicit provenance.
Distinct: upgrading an old-version message emits an observable event so the source can be notified to upgrade its client schema.

removed.reason: Settled schema-header structural detail (schemas have ordinary, owner, Sema headers) now living in schema/code; not a durable load-bearing arrow.

survivors.desc: rkyv binary is the single encoded form living both in the database (sema body at rest) and on the wire (component messaging); one AssembledSchema reads bytes in both. NOTA is the text projection at the CLI/inspection edge. Tests prove both boundaries, not only in-memory Rust types.
survivors.reason: Both state the rkyv-at-rest-and-on-wire vs NOTA-at-edge split; 0wt5 adds the test-both-boundaries clause.

survivors.desc: rkyv is the universal wire base for the schema-derived stack; the NOTA codec (NotaDecode/NotaEncode + derive) is opt-in per consumer. DOUBLE clients (text+binary, e.g. CLI) carry the NOTA derive; binary-only clients (daemon) omit it for leanness and to structurally reject NOTA text at the wire. Same data types across consumers — only the derive set differs; byte-compatibility comes from the identical shared rkyv layout. Schema-emitted types derive both NOTA and rkyv from one .asschema, with extra communication traits on roots that cross component boundaries, reusing the same NOTA typed interfaces as direct Rust rather than a parallel mini-codec.
survivors.reason: All three cover one arrow: schema-emitted types derive both NOTA and rkyv from shared interfaces, opt-in NOTA derive per consumer, same data shape across clients.

survivors.desc: The assembled schema (.asschema) is canonical NOTA-and-rkyv only, round-tripped through AsschemaNotaWriter/Reader. All NOTA output comes from the typed codec — a printed type label is a real decodable shape, never hand-rolled witness/line formats or ad hoc field joins; a known-root file is its root body encoded through an object/body abstraction over ordered fields.
survivors.reason: k27m (no manual string formatting pretending to be NOTA) and hhaf (no ad hoc field joins on known roots) are the affirmative codec rule; hc0t is the strong anti-witness-format constraint. One arrow about typed-codec-only NOTA output.

survivors.desc: Spirit record privacy is an Optional field available to any record kind: a public record's privacy is None, an elevated one is (Some Magnitude). Optionality is the explicit None token, never an omitted positional field — every NOTA positional record carries every field.
survivors.reason: j3bq (privacy on any kind) is subsumed into ypb5's privacy-as-Optional record.

survivors.desc: NOTA encoders avoid over-bracketing: bare-safe atoms encode bare inside typed positions like vectors, bracket forms reserved for whitespace/delimiter safety; typed projection drops redundant wrapper delimiters when the enclosing head already fixes the payload shape, rendering operation-root fields directly.
survivors.reason: 3naf explicitly refines t6s1; both are the no-redundant-delimiter encoding rule.
Distinct definition: SEMA is the compact data format defined by schema.

survivors.desc: Signal is NOTA-free: signal-frame and the signal-component / meta-signal-component contract crates carry binary/rkyv only, with no NOTA encoding or projection on request/reply types. NOTA is a separate text-edge concern living outside signal.
survivors.reason: Unique still-load-bearing boundary: signal layer is binary-only, NOTA outside it.

survivors.desc: Component feedback, status, and errors are typed self-descriptive NOTA enums and structs whose names carry the meaning — a result type named well enough to need no message string. Our feedback language is enums/structs decoded through NOTA, specializing strings-only-at-the-edges to the messaging surface.
survivors.reason: Unique arrow: typed self-describing feedback, not string messages.

survivors.desc: One typed noun per semantic object across the schema/NOTA stack: source text and checked-in/serialized artifact share the same datatype unless there's a real semantic projection. Assembled schema is one typed Asschema object projected to rkyv bytes (SEMA db), binary archive, and .asschema NOTA text via the same Nota-derived types; a SchemaMacro source entry fits one serializable enum variant, not split source/data enums. Schema source itself is a typed value with a dedicated in/out codec reading strict NOTA source into source-node data and writing it back canonically, distinct from raw NOTA parsing and from assembled serialization.
survivors.reason: rmb8 is the general rule (don't split source vs serialized datatypes); h053 (Asschema), qat2 (SchemaMacro) are instances; ji0f gives schema source its typed codec.

survivors.desc: A loaded schema file's root struct shape is known a priori from the root KIND (base vs reactive) signaled outside the bytes (extension/load context), like a Nix derivation. The file supplies only positional VALUES at known slots — no root, field, or type names. A base file is just its namespace; a reactive file is bare input enum, output enum, namespace, no Input/Output labels even in root.schema.
survivors.reason: Unique known-root design arrow; condensed.

survivors.desc: NOTA mirrors the rkyv self-describing root + relative-pointer box layout: the root stays compact (sized fields inline) while unsized/growing fields (String, Vec, Option-of-unsized, nested records) become BOXES appended after the root in declaration order, so no box-index naming is needed. Coordinate (vector-N element-M) reads the M-th element of the N-th box vector, a direct projection of the binary form.
survivors.reason: Unique structural arrow: NOTA-mirrors-rkyv box layout with coordinate access.

survivors.desc: Canonical name for the 64-bit per-message prefix is short header; every message carries it, everything else is body. Structure is 8 enums (1 root + 7 sub) filling 64 bits, discriminator info only — payload lives in the body. Version is not in the header: the engine enforces it at the database level (one schema version per database; migration changes the database, not per-message tags).
survivors.reason: Canonical naming + structure + version-at-db-level for the short header.

survivors.desc: Identifier design treats the shortest reliable identifier as a first-class target: opaque content-addressed hashes (blake3/sha) tokenize near one token per character, a recurring cost in agent context, logs, and reports. The full digest stays canonical identity; the exposed locator is a stable shortened form, never renamed once exposed.
survivors.reason: Unique short-locator-over-full-hash arrow.

survivors.desc: Spirit operations support a variant ladder: short forms with summary defaults for common operations, complex forms with full metadata for precise control. Both coexist as distinct wire operation roots; the complex root is canonical and the short root expands to a default of it.
survivors.reason: Unique simple-to-complex operation ladder arrow.

survivors.desc: Short-header / signal-type structure: a 64-bit micro-message header (1 root enum + 7 sub-enums) discriminates any object in the namespace in constant time, emitted by every signal contract macro; byte 0 is the ROOT VERB (most universal beingness — Help/Query/Message), bytes 1-7 are the verb-namespace sub-variants. In schema the header is a positional ordered vector of variants [Variant ...]; data-carrying variants (parenthesized name+data) take the first seven slots (0-6) and unit variants (bare PascalCase) follow, so adding a unit variant is a no-op wire upgrade. Root-level variants are limited, nested ones broad. Sub-enums default to one byte (256 variants) but pack tighter by inner-type semantics — Bool in 1 bit, Option in 2, a 16-variant enum in 4 bits — letting SEMA fit smaller than raw rkyv; freed bits encode deeper variant nesting, and multi-byte sub-enums cover >256 variants without exceeding 64 bits. Integers are typed values, not sub-enum kinds. Three sizing tiers: (1) 64-bit micro header, (2) 64-byte summary message (hand-implemented from natural summary fields, basis for a subscription tier), (3) full unrestricted rkyv message.
survivors.reason: All eight circle one design: the 64-bit short-header / signal-type structure — enum count, verb-at-root, slot/variant assignment, root-vs-nested breadth, bit-packing by inner type, and the three sizing tiers.

survivors.desc: Common workspace identifiers (record, forward, send, etc.) are stored as enum-encoded composite names; the wire form is the discriminator, not the string, so the namespace is composable across components and enables multilingual labels.
survivors.reason: Unique: enum-encoded composite names enable composable/multilingual namespace.
Distinct: contract schemas as explicit content-addressable layout schemas for rkyv migration.

survivors.desc: Schema variant namespaces reserve explicit numeric ranges so the encoded form reflects the logical split before deeper payload parsing. Input and Output are partitions of one wire tag space (e.g. a 1-byte ~240-slot enum split roughly two-thirds: ~120 input then ~120 output slots — illustrative, larger enums get larger spaces): the author declares Input and Output separately at the schema level but rkyv merges them into one tag space, so a single tag byte identifies both the variant and its direction, the basis for input/output dispatch at the message layer.
survivors.reason: fry8 (reserved numeric ranges per logical space) is the general rule; x2su (input/output as partitions of one tag space) is the concrete instance.

survivors.desc: Assembled schema reserves scalar pass-through type references for built-in scalars (String, U64, Boolean and similar Rust primitive/domain-floor types) so the assembled form writes the scalar directly instead of wrapping it as Plain(String). Those reserved names are unavailable for user-defined data types.
survivors.reason: Unique: reserved scalar pass-through type references in assembled schema.

survivors.desc: The root schema-generated signal object carries the signal-frame protocol behavior for rkyv serialization and process-to-process dispatch, integrating caller/process-origin support when that library is ready.
survivors.reason: Unique: generated signal root carries signal-frame protocol behavior.

survivors.desc: TraceEvent is a transparent newtype or direct ObjectName trace root when its only payload is the activated object name, yielding a single object shape rather than noisy double-delimited NOTA from a one-field struct.
survivors.reason: Unique still-relevant shape rule for TraceEvent / single-field NOTA newtypes.

survivors.desc: Universal data variants (U8, U16, possibly more primitives) are pre-allocated across all signal namespaces; every namespace inherits them as primitive sub-variants. Uses: Criome's 16-bit short ID for its public key (human-readable in logs), generic counters, small fixed-size values shared across components.
survivors.reason: Unique: universal pre-allocated primitive data variants across namespaces.

survivors.desc: The assembled-schema visibility wrapper is a normal NOTA data-carrying variant: (Public Name Value) and (Private Name Value), with Public/Private as the variant head followed by name and value fields.
survivors.reason: Unique concrete NOTA shape for the visibility wrapper.

survivors.desc: Cloud's Plan operation is renamed Mutate, its acknowledged-state reply Mutated. The daemon distinguishes Mutate-sent (request issued to provider, pending) from Mutated (provider-acknowledged); held state is last-known-acknowledgment, not a live provider query.
survivors.reason: Both records define the Mutate/Mutated rename and its pending-vs-acknowledged semantics — one arrow about cloud mutation state.
Distinct root pattern: Criome components share state by constant mutual acknowledgment (quorum-of-agreement), external providers excepted until federated.

survivors.desc: The lojix deploy daemon serves connections concurrently (bounded by a permit cap), never blocking on in-progress nix builds; deploy state is per-request (each connection owns its pipeline cursor), the durable Store is the shared point locked briefly per sema op, and long nix effects hold no global lock.
survivors.reason: Unique concurrency model for the lojix deploy daemon; condensed from mini-essay, stripping the audit-29 narration.
High-importance unique arrow: implicit origin-route return-address metadata for round-trip reply correlation.

survivors.desc: Communicate is the wire trait between any two components, over binary rkyv with signal-frame (connection setup, async unique IDs for request-reply correlation, handshake). A universal mail-queue manager commits intent on accept; the reply carries a database marker (hash plus counter) so clients verify the transaction and guarantee local-state consistency against the daemon's authoritative database.
survivors.reason: Defines the Communicate trait, signal-frame, mail queue, and database-marker reply guarantee — one wire-interface arrow, condensed.
Distinct consensus discipline: schema-defined execution consults state and trusted owner/core authority before mutating; single-owner avoids races.

survivors.desc: Nexus output is slim: input is a query/decision, output a result/what-happened acknowledgement. Clients issue follow-up queries for specific state rather than receiving everything; the wire contract stays compact and identifier-shaped, with detail living in the engine.
survivors.reason: Unique slim-acknowledgement arrow at the Nexus level; condensed.

survivors.desc: The persona system is federated at its core: each persona triad (mind + orchestrator + spirit) is one federated unit. Orchestrators across personas and namespaces become aware of subtasks they send, interfacing via skilled-agent subtasks; each persona offers itself as a context-bearing thing whose opinion can be queried, making federation natural rather than special-cased.
survivors.reason: Unique federation-of-personas arrow; lightly condensed.

survivors.desc: domain-criome is the authority for .criome domains — a content-addressed DNS where each domain (e.g. goldragon.criome) is its own content-addressable authority server queried via its daemon, last-snapshot as cached state. A daemon may host multiple domains and resolve ones it doesn't own from agreed last-known cached records; domain-criome decides node ownership, with NotAuthoritative(Delegation) redirect as the off-network fallback.
survivors.reason: Both define domain-criome as the .criome authority with content-addressed resolution, caching, and delegation fallback — one DNS arrow.

survivors.desc: On next-version failure (catastrophic or partial-support), main recovers caller intent: it reconstructs what it can from the original message via partial application and records the divergence, treating partial support the same as cant-do-at-all to preserve caller intent across version divergence.
survivors.reason: Both cover main's recovery of caller intent across next/version-divergence failures (catastrophic and partial) — one fault-tolerance arrow.

survivors.desc: LLM calls are computation units configured as a default provider plus an ordered fallback-provider chain tried in sequence, with per-provider config tweaks evaluated at the typed daemon boundary. The daemon library is the reducer plus checker, including authorization that can escalate to Criome — giving failure-tolerance and provider portability.
survivors.reason: Unique provider-fallback-chain arrow for LLM calls; condensed.

survivors.desc: Public-traffic routing uses Persona-orchestrated FD handoff via SCM_RIGHTS (Design D) for atomic lossless cutover: clients connect to ONE stable public socket per component; Persona binds it, accepts connections, hands accepted FDs to the active-version daemon over a per-component control connection, then stays off the byte path. Same socket model in dev and prod.
survivors.reason: ns7t adopts Persona FD-handoff (Design D) and n2te rejects client-side discovery for one stable Persona-managed socket — same routing decision; absorbs bb83's atomic-cutover-by-protocol claim, whose lossless/atomic cutover purpose is exactly this mechanism.
Distinct signal-engine role definition: triage/admission/dispatch only, no heavy logic.

removed.reason: Settled implementation detail of the spirit-next 408 prototype (specific method names, deleted vestigial code, type-enforcement mechanics) now living in code; transient prototype state. The signal->nexus->sema ordering arrow it relied on is preserved by u7fj (signal-engine role) and 07pn (origin-route through signal/nexus/sema).

Low-certainty illustrative feature note (VM-testing node reachable via a Criome domain) — implementation detail extending the domain-criome registry/resolution already covered by crlc/ty24, no distinct durable arrow.

survivors.desc: Daemon has three execution centers whose traits encode order: Signal (messaging; first gate, rejects invalid messages back to client), Nexus (mail keeper + translator; holds mail being-processed, converts Signal<->SEMA), SEMA (state). Push only on success; push methods take the next engine as a parameter, sequencing Signal->Nexus->SEMA then reversing. Nexus may host an internal recursive engine for future runtime control.
survivors.reason: o8x5 (Nexus role + full flow + three execution centers), g3f2 (three engines whose traits define order, push-on-success), daht (Signal first gate), 4vi4 (Nexus internal recursive engine for future runtime control) all describe the one Signal/Nexus/SEMA pipeline arrow. Desc trimmed to ~330 chars from ~640.

Distinct arrow: Nexus has its own typed NexusDecision/NexusEffect decision language for non-SEMA decisions (Stash, Fanout, Drop, Enqueue, Preempt) with generated runner loop. Adds the typed-effect-language fact not in the pipeline merge. Kept desc empty per desc_changed=false.

survivors.desc: The wire protocol is the Signal Protocol; its messages move through a universal mail mechanism (mailer/dispatcher/push system) shared by every component. Asynchronous representation lives at the data-type level: message data types themselves carry correlation IDs and lifecycle state tracking sent/queued/processing/replied transitions.
survivors.reason: jl3k (Signal Protocol name + universal mail mechanism) and fc3j (async representation at data-type level: correlation IDs + lifecycle state) describe the same Signal-Protocol-as-typed-async-wire arrow.

survivors.desc: Message lifecycle exposes hookable events: the mail dispatch system emits/commits a typed MessageSent action at the message-sent boundary, firing as soon as a message is sent so hooks, UI, observers, routers, and subscribers can react immediately.
survivors.reason: q73w (method-on-message-sent hook fires + commits through mail dispatch) and kgl2 (MessageSent typed action as event surface for hooks/UI/routers/subscribers) are the same on-sent event-surface arrow.

survivors.desc: Every actor has its own schema (a channel-contract) declaring base enums: ACTION (what it can do) and RESPONSE (what it can say), plus a universal Unknown response for unrecognized requests. Execution matches schema variants to select replies and route work. Execution is fan-out: one interaction-actor decision emits multiple parallel outputs (effects, replies, messages to other actors), closed per interact-trait variant.
survivors.reason: 8y24 (per-actor ACTION/RESPONSE enums + Unknown), gmlv (schema base enums matched in execution), v91q (fan-out single-input-multiple-output, closed per-variant outputs) all describe the per-actor-schema/actor-execution arrow. Desc trimmed from ~570 chars.

survivors.desc: The schema declares an EFFECT TABLE: a closed mapping from message types to effect types, with replies declared per message. Dispatch is structural match through the interact-trait contact point (message -> mapped effect -> mapped reply); effects, messaging, and interactions are all mapped, and internal work composes them. Match always; map always; match rather than compute.
survivors.reason: Distinct arrow: the closed schema-declared effect table and match-driven (not ad-hoc/string/boolean) dispatch. De-negated to affirmative.

survivors.desc: message and router stay separate (the SO_PEERCRED trust boundary cannot move into router). message owns the EXISTENCE fact — an authenticated message authored and witnessed at the SO_PEERCRED ingress — as a durable event it emits. router is authoritative for delivery on the routed gated-or-remote path (durable on the harness channel ack). Direct-delivery fast path: addressing a publicly-reachable local agent by uid delivers peer-to-peer without router, establishing delivery on the target's direct ack.
survivors.reason: l3k4 (router authoritative delivery on harness ack; message logs existence), alom (Option A: message owns durable existence event, components separate), i99x (direct-delivery fast path superseding l3k4 cl.2 and 17ss for direct path), 17ss (router-ack delivery durable) are one evolving message/router existence-vs-delivery arrow. i99x is current state; merged with clauses preserved. Desc slightly over 280 to keep both delivery paths.

survivors.desc: Streaming is full schema-derived push (not poll-as-permanent, not lojix-only): teach schema-next an event/stream root with opens/belongs relations; teach schema-rust-next to emit the event frame into signal-frame's StreamingFrameBody + ObservableSet pub-sub; add push action and subscriber registry to triad-runtime. signal-lojix carries WatchDeployments/WatchCacheRetention from day one; until push lands, handshake+poll suffices. SubscribePolicy default TerminateAtHandover: subscriptions end at handover, clients reconnect to next.
survivors.reason: brgo (full schema-derived streaming direction + substrate plan), 2tfa (signal-lojix carries Watch subscriptions day one), s8es (SubscribePolicy TerminateAtHandover default) are the one streaming-subscription arrow. Desc over 280 to hold all three sub-arrows.

removed.reason: Deferral note (backpressure and deeper runtime-control are future work) — settled scoping decision now subsumed by survivor 1, where 4vi4 already records Nexus as the future runtime-control substrate. No unique still-relevant arrow vanishes; the scoping fact is task/phase state, not durable intent.

survivors.desc: Intent capture is modeled as a court of law: the submitting agent advocates, psyche verbatim quotes (asterisk-bracketed spans) are testimony, the guardian is judge rendering a binary verdict. The justification is a strongly-typed argued case (typed testimony with optional antecedents plus reasoning for why certainty/domain/kind/importance fit), never a stringly blob; the claim is the operation's own Entry. Certainty is the burden of proof the quotes must clear on modal strength: over-claimed certainty is rejected back to the highest-thinking model to reword, the guardian never silently lowers it, preserving psyche modality. Ship the lean core first (typed testimony plus support check, binary verdict), deferring the elaborate court structure until it demonstrably falls short.
survivors.reason: All six are one arrow: the court-of-law intent-justification protocol — model, justification structure, certainty-as-burden, modality preservation, lean-core phasing.

survivors.desc: The guardian model performs semantic judgment on every guarded write while deterministic code gathers context, validates structure, and applies typed verdict consequences. It judges a submission across justification-read dimensions (does it justify the intent, is the domain right, do certainty and importance fit), and judges a cross-record operation as a whole in one yes/no. Supersede must atomically replace a retired set with multiple new intents; the guardian asks whether the new set together preserves the kernel. Each operation type gets a per-operation prompt with worked accept/reject examples (over-train first, then scale back), and must always return well-formed NOTA via a generated verdict grammar, closed rejection set, parse-and-retry, and shape test. A specialized clean-context trained guardian outperforms the noise-buried submitting agent even on a weaker model; specialization beats raw model strength.
survivors.reason: All cover guardian design/behavior: what it judges, multi-replacement supersede, per-operation training, well-formed-NOTA guarantee, specialization-beats-model-strength, judgment/deterministic split.
Distinct arrow: no NOTA between components; binary protocol data with CLI as the debugging translation surface.
Distinct naming decision: policy/control socket is the meta socket, not owner socket.
Distinct optimization: skip guardian for already-registered referent; registry state settles it.
Distinct fail-closed safety arrow: Spirit without configured guardian rejects ungated writes.

survivors.desc: Sema classification vocabulary stays internal to sema-engine execution and observation, off the public contract wire. Contract operation roots are domain verbs and the Sema class is daemon-derived internally; the six Sema words (Assert, Mutate, Retract, Match, Subscribe, Validate) appear neither as request-root tags nor as an AuthorizedSignalVerb enum nor as a payloadless SemaObservation event in any signal/meta-signal contract. Requires a cleanup pass across the legacy contracts carrying the pattern (signal-mind, signal-router, signal-criome, owner-signal-persona, signal-persona-spirit, signal-orchestrate).
survivors.reason: Distinct firm prohibition: Sema vocabulary off the public contract wire.
Distinct concrete transport decision for the version-control mirror: tailnet TCP listener, typed peer identity, ssh-forwarded sockets rejected.

survivors.desc: The wire-header pattern grows by extension not replacement: the 8-byte Tier 1 micro-type header is the base and lives as a parseable prefix inside longer (256/512/variable) headers holding most of the frame structure; a header declares an enum identifier plus ordered endpoint nodes and encodes enough top-level variant shape for cheap routing. Schema declares which fields fit in 8 bytes vs extended positions; data vectors follow as memory arrays the header indexes. Every message carries the 64-bit micro-type marker (type, size, root structure), rkyv zero-copy after a network-fingerprint check, with the prefix doubling as a default-on tap-anywhere observation channel. An extended 512/64-byte Tier 2 sits between the micro variant and the full rkyv record for payloads needing public keys, identities, signatures, or short descriptions (e.g. a Criome quorum authorization payload).
survivors.reason: One arrow: the tiered extensible short-header wire format — base 8-byte prefix, extended tiers/header growth, rkyv zero-copy, tap-anywhere observation, header content/layout. Removed dx10 (its agent-short-ID arrow is distinct, now its own survivor).

survivors.desc: Each launched role/agent receives a short identifier derived from the first three bytes of its Criome master-identity public key, exposed via a shortest_id() method on the full ID that returns the shortest non-colliding prefix (3 bytes default, widening up to the NOTA namespace width on collision). Because identities are binary in the signal layer, the collision check is a direct byte comparison on the public-key prefix.
survivors.reason: RESTORED: the first pass wrongly merged dx10 into the wire-header arrow and duplicated it as a placeholder. Its real arrow — agent short identifiers from Criome public-key prefix with shortest_id() collision-widening — is distinct and was otherwise lost.

survivors.desc: The 64-bit signal root-verb namespace is per-component, not workspace-wide: byte 0 is each channel's own root-verb enum (up to 256 verbs), keyed for decoding by provenance (which channel/socket the log came from), while universal data variants (U8, U16) stay shared in bytes 1-7. The namespace is partitioned into a SignalCore system-types zone and a component-specific zone with pre-allocated sizes; repartitioning is a major-version event. Byte 0 may split by golden ratio (~0.39/0.61) between owner and public contracts (default owner-small, public-big), enforced at compile time (both contracts must agree or compilation fails), opening a single-socket-per-component design where byte 0 encodes the namespace.
survivors.reason: One arrow: byte-0 signal root-verb namespace partitioning — per-component, zoned, golden-ratio owner/public split.

survivors.desc: Root signal shapes should begin with an enum differentiating input and output spaces: reserve variant-number ranges over an 8-bit root enum so one first-byte header distinguishes request-like input variants from response-like output variants for the same actor mechanism, keeping dispatch cheap.
survivors.reason: Both express the input/output split via reserved variant ranges in the root enum.
Distinct arrow: a sema-side short header symmetric with the signal-side, same macro machinery, tap-anywhere on engine ops.

survivors.desc: Signal-frame protocol (framing, short-header dispatch, route-enum derivation, codec object) belongs declaratively in a root-level signal-frame.schema imported by component schemas, which inherit frame methods on their Input/Output surfaces — schema-as-protocol-substrate replacing hand-rolled transport route tables and frame codecs. The Spirit full-schema branch keeps public and permissioned sockets as separate signal schemas (equivalent to current ordinary/owner contracts) generated from schema files rather than the legacy signal macro surface.
survivors.reason: Both express schema-declared signal-frame as protocol substrate, including the Spirit full-schema branch's separate-socket schemas.
Distinct arrow: a daemon may expose multiple signal surfaces; configuration is another typed signal surface in the root enumerator.

survivors.desc: Spirit keeps separate ordinary and owner sockets for now, permissions enforced by filesystem socket access under trusted local development (a future general socket with explicit permission prefix is possible but not the current target). Parallel daemon versions need versioned sockets. The multi-version dispatch protocol routes the CLI to the next-version daemon, which coordinates back with main; a missing-next returns an error logged as failed. Upgrade orders triggering the smart handover arrive through the target component's owner socket. PeerCheck is retired — signal-version-handover is the single discovery mechanism for next-version daemons.
survivors.reason: One arrow: socket/version layout for parallel daemons — separate ordinary/owner sockets, versioned sockets, multi-version dispatch, handover via owner socket, version-handover as sole discovery.
Distinct full-pipeline arrow: Signal->Nexus->SEMA->Nexus->Signal, async with rolling origin identifiers routing responses including partial multi-op replies.
Distinct arrow: Criome authorization contracts as content-addressed composable objects with threshold/majority/time-window policies layered on top.

removed.reason: Settled fine-grained wire detail (replies should not repeat operation kind) now living in schema/code; no durable distinct guidance.
Terse settled contract-vocabulary note (domain-criome provider-neutral, record-only); subsumed by general contract-vocabulary discipline.
Mis-logged task order: audit every contract repo for incorrectly-carried Nexus/SEMA surfaces. Durable principle captured by 7l7l; this is the work order.
Low-certainty operational UX note (Pi operator-safety should not create repeated confirmation burden); not a durable protocol arrow.
